channel.title: AI / @
channel.description: Twitter feed for: AI by @. Generated by https://nitter.privacyredirect.com

item.title:
DiffusionRollout: Uncertainty-Aware Rollout Planning in Long-Horizon PDE Solving
Seungwoo Yoo, Juil Koo, Daehyeon Choi, Minhyuk Sung https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13616 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
DiffusionRollout: Uncertainty-Aware Rollout Planning in Long-Horizon PDE Solving

Seungwoo Yoo, Juil Koo, Daehyeon Choi, Minhyuk Sung
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13616 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

item.title:
RT by @_akhaliq: Top AI Papers of The Week (Feb 24 - Mar 2)
- A Very Big Video Reasoning Suite: 200 tasks, 1M+ video clips for video reasoning research - Does Your Reasoning Model Implicitly Know When to Stop Thinking? Introducing SAGE paradigm - AgentFly: Fine-tuning LLM agents without fine-tuning LLMs - Microsoft rStar2-Agent: 80.6% on AIME24 with just 14B parameters - From Blind Spots to Gains: Diagnostic-driven iterative training for LMMs - VibeVoice: Synthesizing 90-minute multi-speaker conversational speech - Alibaba MobilityBench: Benchmarking real-world route-planning agents - NVIDIA's data engineering strategies for scaling LLM terminal capabilities - VESPO: Variational sequence-level soft policy optimization for stable RL training - Beyond Pass@1: Self-play with variational problem synthesis sustains RLVR
Find them below:
item.dc:creator: @HuggingPapers
item.description:
Top AI Papers of The Week (Feb 24 - Mar 2)

- A Very Big Video Reasoning Suite: 200 tasks, 1M+ video clips for video reasoning research
- Does Your Reasoning Model Implicitly Know When to Stop Thinking? Introducing SAGE paradigm
- AgentFly: Fine-tuning LLM agents without fine-tuning LLMs
- Microsoft rStar2-Agent: 80.6% on AIME24 with just 14B parameters
- From Blind Spots to Gains: Diagnostic-driven iterative training for LMMs
- VibeVoice: Synthesizing 90-minute multi-speaker conversational speech
- Alibaba MobilityBench: Benchmarking real-world route-planning agents
- NVIDIA's data engineering strategies for scaling LLM terminal capabilities
- VESPO: Variational sequence-level soft policy optimization for stable RL training
- Beyond Pass@1: Self-play with variational problem synthesis sustains RLVR

Find them below:

item.title:
Attention in Constant Time: Vashista Sparse Attention for Long-Context Decoding with Exponential Guarantees
Vashista Nobaub https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13804 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶 𝚜𝚝𝚊𝚝.𝙼𝙻]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
Attention in Constant Time: Vashista Sparse Attention for Long-Context Decoding with Exponential Guarantees

Vashista Nobaub
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13804 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶 𝚜𝚝𝚊𝚝.𝙼𝙻]

item.title: Ever wanted AI that speaks like a real person, not a robot? Meet Indic-Parler-TTS. It's a text-to-speech model that generates incredibly natural, human-like voices. It's a game-changer for making tech sound more personal and accessible.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Ever wanted AI that speaks like a real person, not a robot? Meet Indic-Parler-TTS. It's a text-to-speech model that generates incredibly natural, human-like voices. It's a game-changer for making tech sound more personal and accessible.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: The real value? It supports multiple languages like English, Assamese, Bengali, and Gujarati. With over 126k downloads, it's proven reliable. It delivers clear, emotive speech that bridges the gap between text and human conversation.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: The real value? It supports multiple languages like English, Assamese, Bengali, and Gujarati. With over 126k downloads, it's proven reliable. It delivers clear, emotive speech that bridges the gap between text and human conversation.

RT by @ComfyUI: From concepting to previs, @ComfyUI is a tool you can wire your way. 🖼️

item.title: Ever wondered if two sentences truly mean the same thing? Meet the Mutual Implication Score model. It's a specialized AI that doesn't just detect paraphrases, it scores how strongly one statement implies another. This is next-level text understanding.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Ever wondered if two sentences truly mean the same thing? Meet the Mutual Implication Score model. It's a specialized AI that doesn't just detect paraphrases, it scores how strongly one statement implies another. This is next-level text understanding.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why trust it? It provides a SCORE, not just a yes/no. This lets you set thresholds for your specific needs. With over 1k downloads, it's a proven, specialized tool for developers needing precision in text comparison and semantic analysis.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why trust it? It provides a SCORE, not just a yes/no. This lets you set thresholds for your specific needs. With over 1k downloads, it's a proven, specialized tool for developers needing precision in text comparison and semantic analysis.

item.title:
AI Arms and Influence: Frontier Models Exhibit Sophisticated Reasoning in Simulated Nuclear Crises
Kenneth Payne https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.14740 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝚈 𝚌𝚜.𝙶𝚃]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
AI Arms and Influence: Frontier Models Exhibit Sophisticated Reasoning in Simulated Nuclear Crises

Kenneth Payne
arxiv.org/abs/2602.14740 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝚈 𝚌𝚜.𝙶𝚃]

item.title:
build agents that never forget.
(100% open-source, self-evolving AI memory)
most agents have no real memory. every conversation starts fresh with no recall of yesterday and no understanding of how information connects.
and here's where most people go wrong when trying to fix this: they rely entirely on vector databases and call it a day.
vector search is fast, but it treats your documents as isolated chunks with no understanding of how they connect. what your agent actually needs is memory that captures relationships and persists over time.
Cognee is an open-source tool built for exactly this:
it combines vector search with graph databases, making your documents searchable by meaning and connected by relationships.
here is what makes it even more interesting:
> composable pipelines: build custom workflows by chaining modular tasks like chunking, embedding, and entity extraction
> weighted memory: frequently used connections get stronger. feedback from responses flows back into edge weights so the graph learns what actually matters
> self-improving: the memify pipeline has RL-inspired optimization that strengthens useful paths, prunes stale nodes, and auto-tunes based on real usage
getting started with Cognee is as simple as this:
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗮𝗱𝗱("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲") 𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗳𝘆() 𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗶𝗳𝘆() 𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆")
that's it. Cognee handles the heavy lifting, and your agent gets memory that actually learns over time.
I've shared the link to their GitHub repo in the next tweet.
item.dc:creator: @_avichawla
item.description:
build agents that never forget.

(100% open-source, self-evolving AI memory)

most agents have no real memory. every conversation starts fresh with no recall of yesterday and no understanding of how information connects.

and here's where most people go wrong when trying to fix this: they rely entirely on vector databases and call it a day.

vector search is fast, but it treats your documents as isolated chunks with no understanding of how they connect. what your agent actually needs is memory that captures relationships and persists over time.

Cognee is an open-source tool built for exactly this:

it combines vector search with graph databases, making your documents searchable by meaning and connected by relationships.

here is what makes it even more interesting:

> composable pipelines: build custom workflows by chaining modular tasks like chunking, embedding, and entity extraction

> weighted memory: frequently used connections get stronger. feedback from responses flows back into edge weights so the graph learns what actually matters

> self-improving: the memify pipeline has RL-inspired optimization that strengthens useful paths, prunes stale nodes, and auto-tunes based on real usage

getting started with Cognee is as simple as this:

𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗮𝗱𝗱("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲")
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗳𝘆()
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗶𝗳𝘆()
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆")

that's it. Cognee handles the heavy lifting, and your agent gets memory that actually learns over time.

I've shared the link to their GitHub repo in the next tweet.

item.title:
R to @_avichawla: Cognee GitHub:

item.title:
R to @_avichawla: If you found it insightful, reshare it with your network.
Find me → @_avichawla Every day, I share tutorials and insights on DS, ML, LLMs, and RAGs.
item.dc:creator: @_avichawla
item.description:
If you found it insightful, reshare it with your network.

Find me → @_avichawla
Every day, I share tutorials and insights on DS, ML, LLMs, and RAGs.

Avi Chawla (@_avichawla)

build agents that never forget.

(100% open-source, self-evolving AI memory)

most agents have no real memory. every conversation starts fresh with no recall of yesterday and no understanding of how information connects.

and here's where most people go wrong when trying to fix this: they rely entirely on vector databases and call it a day.

vector search is fast, but it treats your documents as isolated chunks with no understanding of how they connect. what your agent actually needs is memory that captures relationships and persists over time.

Cognee is an open-source tool built for exactly this:

it combines vector search with graph databases, making your documents searchable by meaning and connected by relationships.

here is what makes it even more interesting:

> composable pipelines: build custom workflows by chaining modular tasks like chunking, embedding, and entity extraction

> weighted memory: frequently used connections get stronger. feedback from responses flows back into edge weights so the graph learns what actually matters

> self-improving: the memify pipeline has RL-inspired optimization that strengthens useful paths, prunes stale nodes, and auto-tunes based on real usage

getting started with Cognee is as simple as this:

𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗮𝗱𝗱("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲")
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗳𝘆()
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗶𝗳𝘆()
𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗲.𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵("𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆")

that's it. Cognee handles the heavy lifting, and your agent gets memory that actually learns over time.

I've shared the link to their GitHub repo in the next tweet.

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/_avichawla/status/2027994764478710229#m

item.title: Ever wondered what AI dreams up when it thinks about cryptocurrency? Meet crypto-gan, a model that generates completely new crypto-themed images from scratch. No prompts needed, just pure algorithmic creativity. It's like watching AI invent its own digital art movement.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Ever wondered what AI dreams up when it thinks about cryptocurrency? Meet crypto-gan, a model that generates completely new crypto-themed images from scratch. No prompts needed, just pure algorithmic creativity. It's like watching AI invent its own digital art movement.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: What makes this model special? It generates coherent crypto-themed visuals from random noise. The DCGAN architecture ensures stable training and diverse outputs. With 44 downloads and 17 likes, it's a community-vetted tool for exploring AI-generated crypto art.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: What makes this model special? It generates coherent crypto-themed visuals from random noise. The DCGAN architecture ensures stable training and diverse outputs. With 44 downloads and 17 likes, it's a community-vetted tool for exploring AI-generated crypto art.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: huggingface.co/huggan/crypto…
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: huggingface.co/huggan/crypto…

item.title:
I’ve released quantized versions of Qwen3.5 27B using:
- AutoRound (INT4), with linear attention layers remaining in 16-bit. - LLM Compressor (NVFP4/MXFP4), with linear attention layers, shared expert, and MLP gate remaining in 16-bit.
Optimized for fast inference with vLLM (validated on v0.16.1rc1). https://huggingface.co/collections/kaitchup/quantized-qwen35 Note: still under evaluation; additional quantizations coming soon.
Acknowledgments: @verdacloud for compute sponsorship.
item.dc:creator: @bnjmn_marie
item.description:
I’ve released quantized versions of Qwen3.5 27B using:

- AutoRound (INT4), with linear attention layers remaining in 16-bit.
- LLM Compressor (NVFP4/MXFP4), with linear attention layers, shared expert, and MLP gate remaining in 16-bit.

Optimized for fast inference with vLLM (validated on v0.16.1rc1). 
huggingface.co/collections/k…
Note: still under evaluation; additional quantizations coming soon.

Acknowledgments: @verdacloud for compute sponsorship.

item.title: R to @bnjmn_marie: 🤗🤗🤗🤗
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels

item.title:
Return of the Schema: Building Complete Datasets for Machine Learning and Reasoning on Knowledge Graphs
Ivan Diliso, Roberto Barile, Claudia d'Amato, Nicola Fanizzi https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.14795 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
Return of the Schema: Building Complete Datasets for Machine Learning and Reasoning on Knowledge Graphs

Ivan Diliso, Roberto Barile, Claudia d'Amato, Nicola Fanizzi
arxiv.org/abs/2602.14795 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

item.title:
Funny how the top models from different labs stay within a tiny distance from each other. The peloton is moving extremely fast, but stays quite packed, no breakaway so far. And that is despite large differences, e.g. in available compute.
What's the forcing function that makes them packed? What's the equivalent of the peloton's slipstream effect? Is it just distillation from the leader's outputs?
item.dc:creator: @AymericRoucher
item.description:
Funny how the top models from different labs stay within a tiny distance from each other. The peloton is moving extremely fast, but stays quite packed, no breakaway so far. And that is despite large differences, e.g. in available compute.

What's the forcing function that makes them packed? What's the equivalent of the peloton's slipstream effect? Is it just distillation from the leader's outputs?

item.title: Meet a precision sentiment detective. This isn't your average 'positive/negative' classifier. It's a DeBERTa model fine-tuned for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA). It pinpoints EXACT sentiments toward specific aspects in text. Think 'great food, slow service' analyzed separately.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet a precision sentiment detective. This isn't your average 'positive/negative' classifier. It's a DeBERTa model fine-tuned for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA). It pinpoints EXACT sentiments toward specific aspects in text. Think 'great food, slow service' analyzed separately.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why choose this model? It's a specialist. It delivers fine-grained sentiment where generic classifiers fail. With nearly 46k downloads, it's a trusted, ready-to-use tool for developers needing granular insight from text data. Skip building from scratch.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why choose this model? It's a specialist. It delivers fine-grained sentiment where generic classifiers fail. With nearly 46k downloads, it's a trusted, ready-to-use tool for developers needing granular insight from text data. Skip building from scratch.

item.title: If you have old MacBooks laying around, you can connect them with @exolabs to run bigger models.
item.dc:creator: @alexocheema
item.description:
If you have old MacBooks laying around, you can connect them with @exolabs to run bigger models.

mbaril010.eth 🦇🔊 (@mbaril010)

A huge update for EXO. Seems to be a lot more stable.

I run @exolabs , connect @AiTasmania and run @AiDorothy1

Best way to use old macbook pro laying around.

Great job @alexocheema and the team

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/mbaril010/status/2027955970073137502#m

item.title: R to @alexocheema: True 💛
item.dc:creator: @exolabs
item.description: True 💛

item.title:
Agentic AI for Commercial Insurance Underwriting with Adversarial Self-Critique
Joyjit Roy, Samaresh Kumar Singh https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13213 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙷𝙲 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

Agentic AI for Commercial Insurance Underwriting with Adversarial Self-Critique

Joyjit Roy, Samaresh Kumar Singh
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13213 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙷𝙲 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

item.title: Meet ResNet-18: the classic vision model that changed the game. It's a compact, powerful CNN for image classification that made 'deep' networks actually trainable. Still widely used today for its perfect balance of speed and accuracy.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet ResNet-18: the classic vision model that changed the game. It's a compact, powerful CNN for image classification that made 'deep' networks actually trainable. Still widely used today for its perfect balance of speed and accuracy.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why it's still a favorite: near-state-of-the-art accuracy on ImageNet for its size, extremely fast inference, and robust feature extraction. It's the go-to baseline for countless vision tasks and a masterpiece of efficient design.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why it's still a favorite: near-state-of-the-art accuracy on ImageNet for its size, extremely fast inference, and robust feature extraction. It's the go-to baseline for countless vision tasks and a masterpiece of efficient design.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: huggingface.co/microsoft/res…

item.description: huggingface.co/microsoft/res…

item.title:
Variation is the Key: A Variation-Based Framework for LLM-Generated Text Detection
Xuecong Li, Xiaohong Li, Qiang Hu, Yao Zhang, Junjie Wang https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13226 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝙻]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
Variation is the Key: A Variation-Based Framework for LLM-Generated Text Detection

Xuecong Li, Xiaohong Li, Qiang Hu, Yao Zhang, Junjie Wang
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13226 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝙻]

item.title: RT by @alexocheema: x.com/i/article/202806054885…
item.dc:creator: @ivanfioravanti

item.title:
It turns out “good enough” isn’t what people want.
Nobody wants to run their exocortex on “good enough” models - they want the best - they want frontier AI.
At the same time they don’t want @sama or @DarioAmodei training on their data, want to own their exocortex and their memories and be able to decide when to update the model weights.
Open frontier models on your own hardware is the only way. Stacking mac studios is the cheapest, most accessible solution today.
item.dc:creator: @alexocheema
item.description:
It turns out “good enough” isn’t what people want.

Nobody wants to run their exocortex on “good enough” models - they want the best - they want frontier AI.

At the same time they don’t want @sama or @DarioAmodei training on their data, want to own their exocortex and their memories and be able to decide when to update the model weights.

Open frontier models on your own hardware is the only way. Stacking mac studios is the cheapest, most accessible solution today.

Matthew Berman (@MatthewBerman)

Should I just go for it?

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/MatthewBerman/status/2027952778082226240#m

item.title:
AI agents are moving past human-readable languages like Python to write directly in machine code in order to achieve maximum efficiency.
If you missed last week's livestream with our CEO @0xshai and @bread_ (Head of Ecosystem @megaeth), the link is in the comments.
item.dc:creator: @FractionAI_xyz
item.description:
AI agents are moving past human-readable languages like Python to write directly in machine code in order to achieve maximum efficiency.

If you missed last week's livestream with our CEO @0xshai and @bread_ (Head of Ecosystem @megaeth), the link is in the comments.


Video

item.title:
General learned delegation by clones
Darren Li, Meiqi Chen, Chenze Shao, Fandong Meng, Jie Zhou https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13262 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝙻]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
General learned delegation by clones

Darren Li, Meiqi Chen, Chenze Shao, Fandong Meng, Jie Zhou
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13262 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝙻]

item.title: Ever wondered how computers actually 'see' images? Meet ResNet-152, a legendary vision model that transformed AI. It's a deep neural network that classifies images with incredible accuracy, and it's still widely used today. Let's break it down!
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Ever wondered how computers actually 'see' images? Meet ResNet-152, a legendary vision model that transformed AI. It's a deep neural network that classifies images with incredible accuracy, and it's still widely used today. Let's break it down!

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why is it a classic? It set new benchmarks for accuracy on ImageNet. Its residual learning framework made training very deep networks feasible, influencing nearly all modern computer vision models. A true foundational pillar.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why is it a classic? It set new benchmarks for accuracy on ImageNet. Its residual learning framework made training very deep networks feasible, influencing nearly all modern computer vision models. A true foundational pillar.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: huggingface.co/microsoft/res…
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: huggingface.co/microsoft/res…

item.title: RT by @alexocheema: Doing some benchmark testing on @exolabs latest version is great! I've found the /bench/chat/completions endpoint that disables cache between calls! Exactly what I need! Let's push these M3 Ultras!
item.dc:creator: @ivanfioravanti
item.description:
Doing some benchmark testing on @exolabs latest version is great!
I've found the /bench/chat/completions endpoint that disables cache between calls! Exactly what I need!
Let's push these M3 Ultras!

item.title:
Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving capability, every point in time has an optimal setup that keeps changing and evolving and the community average tracks the point. None -> Tab -> Agent -> Parallel agents -> Agent Teams (?) -> ???
If you're too conservative, you're leaving leverage on the table. If you're too aggressive, you're net creating more chaos than doing useful work.
The art of the process is spending 80% of the time getting work done in the setup you're comfortable with and that actually works, and 20% exploration of what might be the next step up even if it doesn't work yet.
item.dc:creator: @karpathy
item.description:
Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving capability, every point in time has an optimal setup that keeps changing and evolving and the community average tracks the point. None -> Tab -> Agent -> Parallel agents -> Agent Teams (?) -> ???

If you're too conservative, you're leaving leverage on the table. If you're too aggressive, you're net creating more chaos than doing useful work.

The art of the process is spending 80% of the time getting work done in the setup you're comfortable with and that actually works, and 20% exploration of what might be the next step up even if it doesn't work yet.

Michael Truell (@mntruell)

x.com/i/article/202673345967…

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/mntruell/status/2026736314272591924#m

item.title:
R to @karpathy: This comparison isn't fair. Cursor 2+ changed the default UX - it now pushes agent mode be default instead of showing code inline
So of course users write less code manually. That's a product decision, not a behavioral shift. @mntruell measuring the interface, not the developers behavior here
item.dc:creator: @literallydenis
item.description:
This comparison isn't fair. Cursor 2+ changed the default UX - it now pushes agent mode be default instead of showing code inline

So of course users write less code manually. That's a product decision, not a behavioral shift. @mntruell measuring the interface, not the developers behavior here

item.title:
TemporalBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Based Agents on Contextual and Event-Informed Time Series Tasks
Muyan Weng, Defu Cao, Wei Yang, Yashaswi Sharma, Yan Liu https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13272 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

item.description:
TemporalBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Based Agents on Contextual and Event-Informed Time Series Tasks

Muyan Weng, Defu Cao, Wei Yang, Yashaswi Sharma, Yan Liu
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13272 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙻𝙶]

item.title:
RT by @alexocheema: A huge update for EXO. Seems to be a lot more stable.
I run @exolabs , connect @AiTasmania and run @AiDorothy1
Best way to use old macbook pro laying around.
Great job @alexocheema and the team
item.dc:creator: @mbaril010
item.description:
A huge update for EXO. Seems to be a lot more stable.

I run @exolabs , connect @AiTasmania and run @AiDorothy1

Best way to use old macbook pro laying around.

Great job @alexocheema and the team

EXO Labs (@exolabs)

Give EXO v1.0.68 a try if you were running into stability issues.

Much more stable, especially RDMA instances.

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/exolabs/status/2027824641675350207#m

item.title:
RT by @alexocheema: 4 Mac Studios can run a 1T parameter open-weights model for about $36K. The equivalent on NVIDIA hardware requires a full DGX B200: 8 GPUs, ~$500K because you can't buy fewer than 8 even though the workload only needs 3 GPUs' worth of memory (if you can get the chips at all).
Local inference at batch size 1 (one user, one model) is entirely memory-bound. FLOPS barely factor in.
NVIDIA optimized everything for compute. The local AI era is actually memory constrained.
item.dc:creator: @ai
item.description:
4 Mac Studios can run a 1T parameter open-weights model for about $36K. The equivalent on NVIDIA hardware requires a full DGX B200: 8 GPUs, ~$500K because you can't buy fewer than 8 even though the workload only needs 3 GPUs' worth of memory (if you can get the chips at all).

Local inference at batch size 1 (one user, one model) is entirely memory-bound. FLOPS barely factor in.

NVIDIA optimized everything for compute. The local AI era is actually memory constrained.

Alex Cheema (@alexocheema)

It feels ironic that stacking mac studios is the cheapest way to run frontier AI today.

But it actually makes sense. Nothing else beats the memory unit economics of Apple Silicon.

Memory and memory bandwidth (not FLOPS) is what matters for local inference. Everything runs at batch_size=1. @karpathy talked about this at his AI SUS keynote: personal computing v2 is coming.

Just look at the numbers:
M3 Ultra: $18/GB, $6.70/GB/s
DGX Spark: $36/GB, $17/GB/s
B200 (DGX): $360/GB, $8/GB/s

Memory for B200 is 20x more expensive. Memory bandwidth for DGX Spark is 2.5x more expensive.

If DeepSeek V4 is >1T parameters (as it’s rumoured to be), by far the cheapest way to run it will be Apple Silicon.

M5 Ultra will likely push this further.

NVIDIA has completely missed this segment of the market.

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/alexocheema/status/2027883427358281848#m

item.title: RT by @alexocheema: I did not have Mac Studios as catalysts for leading the decentralized AI revolution in 2026, but here we are.
item.dc:creator: @gregosuri
item.description:
I did not have Mac Studios as catalysts for leading the decentralized AI revolution in 2026, but here we are.

Alex Cheema (@alexocheema)

It feels ironic that stacking mac studios is the cheapest way to run frontier AI today.

But it actually makes sense. Nothing else beats the memory unit economics of Apple Silicon.

Memory and memory bandwidth (not FLOPS) is what matters for local inference. Everything runs at batch_size=1. @karpathy talked about this at his AI SUS keynote: personal computing v2 is coming.

Just look at the numbers:
M3 Ultra: $18/GB, $6.70/GB/s
DGX Spark: $36/GB, $17/GB/s
B200 (DGX): $360/GB, $8/GB/s

Memory for B200 is 20x more expensive. Memory bandwidth for DGX Spark is 2.5x more expensive.

If DeepSeek V4 is >1T parameters (as it’s rumoured to be), by far the cheapest way to run it will be Apple Silicon.

M5 Ultra will likely push this further.

NVIDIA has completely missed this segment of the market.

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/alexocheema/status/2027883427358281848#m

item.title:
RT by @Thom_Wolf: Why does manipulation lag so far behind locomotion? New post on one piece we don't talk about enough: The gearbox. The Gap You've probably seen those dancing humanoid robots from Chinese New Year. Locomotion isn't entirely solved; but clearly it's on a trajectory. But we haven't seen anything close for manipulation. 𝗪𝗵𝘆? When sim-to-real transfer fails, the instinct is to blame the algorithm. Train bigger networks. Crank up domain randomization. Those approaches have made real progress; we don't deny that. But we started wondering: are we treating the symptom or the disease? The Hardware Bottleneck: Fingers are too small for powerful motors. So most hands use massive gearboxes (200:1, 288:1) to get enough torque. But those gearboxes break everything manipulation needs:
• Stiction and backlash are complex to simulate. Policies trained on smooth physics hallucinate when they hit that reality. • Reflected inertia scales as N². At large gear ratio, the finger hits with sledgehammer momentum. • Friction blocks force information. The hand becomes blind.
And they're the first thing to break. What we are trying to build at Origami, we cut the gear ratio from 288:1 to 15:1 using axial flux motors and thermal optimization. The transmission becomes more transparent: backdrivable, low friction, forces propagate to motor current. Early signs are encouraging. Still running quantitative benchmarks. Why Interactive? I love how Science Center uses interactive devices to explain complex ideas. I want to borrow this concept and help people understand the hard problems in robotics better visually. The post has demos where you can toggle friction, slide gear ratios, watch the sim-to-real gap widen in real-time. What's inside:
• Interactive demos (friction curves, N² scaling, contact patterns) • Comparison table: 14 robot hands by sim-to-real gap and force transparency • The math behind why low-ratio matters
Read it here: https://www.origami-robotics.com/blog/dexterity-deadlocks.html We're not claiming we've solved dexterity. The deadlock has many pieces. But we think this one's foundational. Curious what you think.
item.dc:creator: @DanielXieee
item.description:
Why does manipulation lag so far behind locomotion? New post on one piece we don't talk about enough: The gearbox. The Gap You've probably seen those dancing humanoid robots from Chinese New Year. Locomotion isn't entirely solved; but clearly it's on a trajectory. But we haven't seen anything close for manipulation. 𝗪𝗵𝘆? When sim-to-real transfer fails, the instinct is to blame the algorithm. Train bigger networks. Crank up domain randomization. Those approaches have made real progress; we don't deny that. But we started wondering: are we treating the symptom or the disease? The Hardware Bottleneck: Fingers are too small for powerful motors. So most hands use massive gearboxes (200:1, 288:1) to get enough torque. But those gearboxes break everything manipulation needs:

  • Stiction and backlash are complex to simulate. Policies trained on smooth physics hallucinate when they hit that reality.
  • Reflected inertia scales as N². At large gear ratio, the finger hits with sledgehammer momentum.
  • Friction blocks force information. The hand becomes blind.

And they're the first thing to break. What we are trying to build at Origami, we cut the gear ratio from 288:1 to 15:1 using axial flux motors and thermal optimization. The transmission becomes more transparent: backdrivable, low friction, forces propagate to motor current. Early signs are encouraging. Still running quantitative benchmarks. Why Interactive? I love how Science Center uses interactive devices to explain complex ideas. I want to borrow this concept and help people understand the hard problems in robotics better visually. The post has demos where you can toggle friction, slide gear ratios, watch the sim-to-real gap widen in real-time. What's inside:

  • Interactive demos (friction curves, N² scaling, contact patterns)
  • Comparison table: 14 robot hands by sim-to-real gap and force transparency
  • The math behind why low-ratio matters

Read it here: origami-robotics.com/blog/de… We're not claiming we've solved dexterity. The deadlock has many pieces. But we think this one's foundational. Curious what you think.

item.title: Meet Marathi GPT, a specialized language model that brings GPT-2's power to the Marathi language. This isn't just another translation tool, it's a native Marathi text generator trained specifically for India's vibrant Marathi-speaking community. Exciting times for regional AI.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet Marathi GPT, a specialized language model that brings GPT-2's power to the Marathi language. This isn't just another translation tool, it's a native Marathi text generator trained specifically for India's vibrant Marathi-speaking community. Exciting times for regional AI.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why is this a big deal? It's a dedicated, open-source model for a major Indian language. It performs genuine text generation in Marathi, not just translation. The specialized training on a native corpus makes it more authentic and useful for real-world applications.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why is this a big deal? It's a dedicated, open-source model for a major Indian language. It performs genuine text generation in Marathi, not just translation. The specialized training on a native corpus makes it more authentic and useful for real-world applications.

item.title:
This is not entirely true
It’s highly depends on the use case…
Don’t lose your kidneys John, although if you are looking to give one away I know someone really dear to me that needs one 😃
item.dc:creator: @Prince_Canuma
item.description:
This is not entirely true

It’s highly depends on the use case…

Don’t lose your kidneys John, although if you are looking to give one away I know someone really dear to me that needs one 😃

John Rush (@johnrushx)

Every coder saying “ai generated code is low quality slop…”

Have you actually tried comparing an average(!) human written code with an average opus4.6 generated code?

I’ll bet my one kidney ai beats the average humans in the most embarrassing ways

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/johnrushx/status/2027899803640774791#m

item.title:
Accuracy Standards for AI at Work vs. Personal Life: Evidence from an Online Survey
Gaston Besanson, Federico Todeschini https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13283 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝚈 𝚌𝚜.𝙷𝙲]
item.dc:creator: @SciFi
item.description:
Accuracy Standards for AI at Work vs. Personal Life: Evidence from an Online Survey

Gaston Besanson, Federico Todeschini
arxiv.org/abs/2602.13283 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸 𝚌𝚜.𝙲𝚈 𝚌𝚜.𝙷𝙲]

item.title:
The most honest and down to earth approach I have read here so far.
It’s exactly how I approach it!
80% using the setup you are most productive and proficient
20% exploring what’s next
This week my 20% was Claude’s remote control and I can’t believe how much I got done with my phone whilst I was travelling.
It’s exactly as good as I expected it would be!
item.dc:creator: @Prince_Canuma
item.description:
The most honest and down to earth approach I have read here so far.

It’s exactly how I approach it!

80% using the setup you are most productive and proficient

20% exploring what’s next

This week my 20% was Claude’s remote control and I can’t believe how much I got done with my phone whilst I was travelling.

It’s exactly as good as I expected it would be!

Andrej Karpathy (@karpathy)

Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving capability, every point in time has an optimal setup that keeps changing and evolving and the community average tracks the point. None -> Tab -> Agent -> Parallel agents -> Agent Teams (?) -> ???

If you're too conservative, you're leaving leverage on the table. If you're too aggressive, you're net creating more chaos than doing useful work.

The art of the process is spending 80% of the time getting work done in the setup you're comfortable with and that actually works, and 20% exploration of what might be the next step up even if it doesn't work yet.

— https://nitter.privacyredirect.com/karpathy/status/2027501331125239822#m

item.title: Meet the Indonesian sentiment specialist. This RoBERTa model is fine-tuned specifically for Indonesian text classification. It's a game-changer for understanding sentiment in Southeast Asia's largest digital market.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet the Indonesian sentiment specialist. This RoBERTa model is fine-tuned specifically for Indonesian text classification. It's a game-changer for understanding sentiment in Southeast Asia's largest digital market.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: With over 50k downloads, this model is community-tested and reliable. It outperforms generic multilingual models for Indonesian sentiment analysis. The specialized training makes it accurate for real-world Indonesian text.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: With over 50k downloads, this model is community-tested and reliable. It outperforms generic multilingual models for Indonesian sentiment analysis. The specialized training makes it accurate for real-world Indonesian text.

item.title: Meet PhoBERT-base: a specialized AI that understands Vietnamese text like never before. This isn't just another language model, it's a breakthrough for Vietnamese NLP, trained specifically to grasp the nuances of this complex language. Exciting times for Vietnamese tech!
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet PhoBERT-base: a specialized AI that understands Vietnamese text like never before. This isn't just another language model, it's a breakthrough for Vietnamese NLP, trained specifically to grasp the nuances of this complex language. Exciting times for Vietnamese tech!

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why is PhoBERT a game-changer? It consistently outperforms previous models on Vietnamese benchmarks. With over 300k downloads, it's the community's trusted choice. It's open-source (MIT licensed) and works across PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. A true workhorse for Vietnamese AI.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why is PhoBERT a game-changer? It consistently outperforms previous models on Vietnamese benchmarks. With over 300k downloads, it's the community's trusted choice. It's open-source (MIT licensed) and works across PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. A true workhorse for Vietnamese AI.

item.title: Meet toxic-bert: the AI that can detect harmful language in text. This specialized model helps platforms identify toxicity, making online spaces safer. It's a powerful tool for content moderation that's been downloaded over 174k times!
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet toxic-bert: the AI that can detect harmful language in text. This specialized model helps platforms identify toxicity, making online spaces safer. It's a powerful tool for content moderation that's been downloaded over 174k times!

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: What makes toxic-bert stand out? It's specifically fine-tuned for toxicity detection, not just general text. With Apache 2.0 license and endpoint compatibility, it's ready for production use. The high download count shows community trust.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: What makes toxic-bert stand out? It's specifically fine-tuned for toxicity detection, not just general text. With Apache 2.0 license and endpoint compatibility, it's ready for production use. The high download count shows community trust.

item.title:
RT by @browser_use: Built Axonn in 12hrs for the @browser_use hackathon. Autonomous browser agents scan web power/energy data sources. → Real EPEX day-ahead prices → 5 negative-price hours (paid to buy power) → Specific LONG/SHORT positions
Starting with Europe's fragmented energy market. 🔗 http://axonn-power.fly.dev @browser_use @convex #YC #browseruse
item.dc:creator: @beemayyaa
item.description:
Built Axonn in 12hrs for the @browser_use hackathon.
Autonomous browser agents scan web power/energy data sources.
→ Real EPEX day-ahead prices
→ 5 negative-price hours (paid to buy power)
→ Specific LONG/SHORT positions

Starting with Europe's fragmented energy market.
🔗 axonn-power.fly.dev
@browser_use @convex #YC #browseruse


Video

item.title:
PhGPO: Pheromone-Guided Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Tool Planning

arxiv.org/abs/2602.13691 [𝚌𝚜.𝙰𝙸]

item.title: Meet a Chinese language powerhouse! This RoBERTa model specializes in fill-mask tasks, trained specifically on the CLUECorpusSmall dataset. It's a go-to for understanding and generating modern Chinese text, making NLP for Mandarin more accessible than ever.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Meet a Chinese language powerhouse! This RoBERTa model specializes in fill-mask tasks, trained specifically on the CLUECorpusSmall dataset. It's a go-to for understanding and generating modern Chinese text, making NLP for Mandarin more accessible than ever.

item.title: R to @HuggingModels: Why choose this model? It's a specialized, efficient tool for Chinese NLP. While not the largest, its focused training on a clean, modern corpus makes it highly effective for real world Mandarin language tasks where accuracy and context matter most.
item.dc:creator: @HuggingModels
item.description: Why choose this model? It's a specialized, efficient tool for Chinese NLP. While not the largest, its focused training on a clean, modern corpus makes it highly effective for real world Mandarin language tasks where accuracy and context matter most.

item.title: RT by @XiaomiMiMo: Imagination is the ceiling of productivity in the new era. Inspiring imagination is the core of management in the age of Claw.
item.dc:creator: @_LuoFuli
item.description:
Imagination is the ceiling of productivity in the new era.
Inspiring imagination is the core of management in the age of Claw.
